title: David Lowery's Strange and Beautiful 'A Ghost Story' | The Big Picture (Ep. 17)
format: Sean Fennessey sets the scene on 'A Ghost Story' with Vanity Fair critic Richard Lawson, then talks to David Lowery the week the film opens: the bedsheet ghost he sat on for a decade, a 30-page script, the movie made in secret, and the existential crisis behind it.

films.title: A Ghost Story
films.note: Lawson was at the film's off-the-radar Sundance premiere. Casey Affleck dies early and haunts the home he shared with Rooney Mara as a sheet with eye holes cut out; divisive for its lack of conventional structure, but Lawson found it hopeful. He praises Lowery's warmth over the bleakness he sees in a lot of young filmmakers.

films.title: Pete's Dragon
films.note: Lowery's Disney remake, the film he made just before this one; Lawson likes how he folds a Disney ethos and folkloric quality into work at any budget.

films.title: It Comes at Night
films.note: Lawson's example of the bleak direction young independent filmmakers tend toward, the trend he thinks Lowery refreshingly avoids.

films.title: Ain't Them Bodies Saints
films.note: Cited as evidence Lowery keeps jumping genres while holding onto a consistent core.

films.title: A Ghost Story
films.note: The bedsheet-ghost image had been in his head almost 10 years. He wrote it in a spontaneous burst finishing post on 'Pete's Dragon' (10 pages the first night, 30 by the next day) and wrote running times into scenes so the crew understood a single line of action might hold for a five-minute take. Shot in secret; kept quiet so a failure could be buried. The 1.33:1 frame with vignettes suits a character trapped in a box, and evokes his parents' slide projections.

films.title: St. Nick
films.note: His first feature, written the same dialogue-light way from a 20-page script; his proof to himself that a thin script could yield a feature-length film.

films.title: Pete's Dragon
films.note: The six-month shoot left him worn out and, turning 34, sinking into nihilistic thoughts about mortality: the existential crisis that produced 'A Ghost Story.' Still, he calls it the work he's most proud of.

films.title: Ain't Them Bodies Saints
films.note: He stopped reading his own reviews during its release. He hasn't watched it in four years and doesn't want to.

films.title: The Conjuring 2
films.note: He saw it right before production and flipped for it; the family-haunting sequence became a surprising visual touchstone for the film's language.

films.title: Syndromes and a Century
films.note: The Apichatpong Weerasethakul film he and his cinematographer watched before shooting.

films.title: Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Lives
films.note: He started to rewatch it and stopped, worried they'd get too close to it. The man in a gorilla suit with light-up LED eyes, goofy yet haunting, was a model for their own Halloween-costume spirit.

films.title: The Lost City of Z
films.note: Comes up as he talks about his next film, another David Grann adaptation; he cites James Gray's version as proof Grann's journalism already reads like a finished movie.

films.title: The Beguiled
films.note: He saw the new Sofia Coppola the other night, loved it, and caught himself wondering mid-screening whether it would feel great to him in a year or two.

films.title: Marie Antoinette
films.note: His favorite Coppola and, he says, a masterpiece, though he can't remember whether he felt that on first viewing.

films.title: A Quiet Passion
films.note: One of his recent favorites; Terence Davies's Emily Dickinson film, which he found passionate despite its muted title.

films.title: Nocturama
films.note: Bertrand Bonello's film, which he caught at the Seattle festival and called extraordinarily provocative and well-made.

films.title: Personal Shopper
films.note: His favorite film of the year so far; he suggests it would make a fantastic double feature with 'A Ghost Story.'

Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Li.title: Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Lives
